local_names.romanized_local: Mashroom Masala (Hindi romanized); Kāḷāṉ Macālā (Tamil romanized)

region_of_origin: India (North Indian cuisine); served widely in Singapore
food_subgroup: Cooked vegetable dish with spiced gravy

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific 'mushroom masala' term; closest defensible parent is 'mushroom dish' (FOODON_03311737). Exact match unavailable.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mushrooms and other fungi products — cooked
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers cooked mushroom products; no specific masala sub-code exists. Used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for mushroom masala curry dish. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of mushroom masala is approximately 200 g, representing one side dish or main accompaniment served with rice or Indian bread.

nutrient_source.title: Mushroom curry (cooked) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition) reports values for mushroom curry (cooked with oil and spices). Mushroom masala is a closely related preparation; values adapted from the mushroom curry entry. Macronutrient profile is consistent with the described recipe of mushrooms, onions, tomatoes, oil, and spices. Minor upward adjustment to fat_g (+0.5 g) applied to account for restaurant-style oil usage typical in Singapore North Indian restaurants, consistent with ICMR-NIN guidance on restaurant-prepared dishes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetable curry dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked mushroom and tomato-onion masala composition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for mushroom masala exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on: (1) cooked mushrooms have a GI of approximately 10–15 (University of Sydney GI Database, mushroom entries); (2) tomato-onion masala base contributes moderate carbohydrate with high fibre and acid content, suppressing glycemic response; (3) mixed vegetable curry dishes with oil and spice tempering are consistently reported in the low GI range (GI 30–45) in peer-reviewed literature (Augustin et al., 2015, American Journal of Clinical Nutrition). Composite estimate of GI 35 is conservative and defensible. GL per 200 g serving = (35 × 6.2 × 2.0) / 100 = 4.34, rounded to 4.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~620 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a 200 g restaurant serving of mushroom masala contributes approximately 620 mg sodium (~27% of the 2300 mg daily limit), and should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to manage cumulative daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and moderate dietary fibre (1.8 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mushroom masala has a low glycemic index of approximately 35 and a glycemic load of approximately 4.3 per 200 g serving, making it a suitable vegetable side dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed with low-GI staples such as basmati rice or whole-wheat roti.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.6 g per 100 g), zero cholesterol, and presence of potassium and dietary fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mushroom masala is low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, with potassium (~280 mg per 100 g) and dietary fibre supporting cardiovascular health; however, individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor sodium intake from restaurant-prepared versions, which may use higher oil and salt quantities.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mushroom Masala is a popular North Indian vegetarian dish consisting of button mushrooms cooked in a spiced tomato-onion gravy. The dish is prepared by first tempering whole spices such as cumin seeds in oil, then sautéing onions, ginger, and garlic until golden, followed by the addition of tomatoes and ground spices including turmeric, red chilli powder, coriander powder, and garam masala. Mushrooms are added and simmered until they absorb the flavours of the masala base. The result is a rich, aromatic, moderately spiced curry with a slightly tangy and earthy flavour profile and a tender-firm mushroom texture. In Singapore, mushroom masala is widely served in North Indian restaurants as a main vegetarian dish or side accompaniment to naan, roti, or basmati rice. It is fully vegetarian, vegan, halal, and gluten-free, making it suitable for a broad range of dietary preferences. Nutritionally, mushroom masala is relatively low in calories (~72 kcal per 100 g), low in saturated fat, and provides useful amounts of B vitamins (particularly riboflavin and niacin from mushrooms), potassium, and dietary fibre. Its low glycemic index (~35) makes it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. The primary clinical consideration is sodium content in restaurant-prepared versions, which may be elevated due to added salt and spice blends. This dish is a good source of umami flavour from mushrooms and is often used as a protein-complementing vegetarian option alongside lentil dishes.

Is mushroom masala good for diabetics?

meal_context: Accompaniment to basmati rice or jeera rice

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mushroom curry data (2017 revised edition), which is the closest approved primary source for this dish. No exact 'mushroom masala' entry exists in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo; values are adapted with minor adjustment for restaurant oil usage. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database mushroom entries and peer-reviewed mixed vegetable curry literature. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN mushroom curry entry; minor fat adjustment (+0.5 g per 100 g) applied for restaurant preparation style. 2. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for mushroom masala exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 3. Sodium value (~310 mg per 100 g) is an estimate for restaurant-prepared mushroom masala; home-prepared versions may be lower (~180–220 mg per 100 g). 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: mushroom masala is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; mushrooms contain negligible B12 not detectable at standard reporting thresholds per ICMR-NIN. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: no animal-derived fat is present in this vegan dish. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero: no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation. 7. field survey data preserved for restaurant count and price range context only; not used as nutritional evidence. 8. Price range SGD 10.43–19.50 from field survey data (12 restaurants, April 2026). 9. Added zero-value justifications for vitamin_b12_mcg, cholesterol_mg, and trans_fat_g in notes. Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency.
